name: V8 TurboFan
summary: V8's classical optimizing JIT IR — sea-of-nodes plus JS type-system specialization, inline-cache feedback embedded at call sites, and deoptimization metadata at every speculative operation. Status (as of 2026-04): being progressively replaced by Turboshaft.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
TurboFan is V8's classical optimizing JIT compiler, layered atop a sea-of-nodes IR. On top of the base SoN substrate, TurboFan adds *type system specialization* (Number vs String vs JSObject), inline-cache feedback (call sites embedded with observed types), and deoptimization metadata (every speculative operation knows how to bail to Ignition bytecode if assumptions fail).

Status (as of 2026-04): TurboFan is being progressively replaced by Turboshaft (linear SSA with side tables), an effort the V8 team began in 2022 and that has migrated most pipelines through 2024–2026.

sections.title: Type-system specialization and IC feedback
sections.content:
TurboFan's distinctive contribution over a vanilla sea-of-nodes is the way it threads JS-runtime feedback into the IR. Inline caches at call sites record observed receiver shapes, primitive types, and method targets; TurboFan reads that feedback when constructing IR nodes, producing speculative operations that assume the observed types. Each speculative node carries a deoptimization point describing how to reconstruct interpreter state if the speculation fails.

The trade-off is steep: peak quality on long-running JS, paid for in compile time and IR-graph complexity. The same TurboFan compile that delivers near-native throughput on hot code is too expensive to invoke on transiently-warm functions, which motivated the introduction of Maglev as a mid-tier between Sparkplug and TurboFan.

sections.title: Migration to Turboshaft
sections.content:
The motivation for replacing TurboFan was operational, not analytical. Sea-of-nodes is elegant for global scheduling, but the per-pass cost of operating on the implicit graph dominated TurboFan's compile time on JS workloads. Turboshaft's linear-SSA-with-side-tables shape eliminates global scheduling per pass and gives pass authors predictable iteration behaviour, at parity-or-better code quality.

The lesson generalises: sea-of-nodes is an analytical luxury that JITs can outgrow. Compile-time amortisation matters more for the median JS function than peak code quality.
